explanation:
The requirement is: 'The transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button located on the back side of the ventilator unit to turn it on.' This means that entering (transitioning to) StartUpMode can only occur if the powerButton is pressed. We can formalize this by saying: Globally, whenever the system enters StartUpMode, it must be preceded (or at the same moment) by the powerButton being pressed. In LTL, this is commonly modeled as 'Globally, if the system enters StartUpMode, then previously (in the immediately prior or same time step) the powerButton was pressed.' However, LTL does not have a 'previous' operator. Instead, in LTL over infinite traces, we typically express this as 'it is always the case that StartUpMode only occurs if the powerButton is pressed at that time.' That is: G(StartUpMode -> powerButton). (It means: at all times, if we're in StartUpMode, then the power button was pushed simultaneously.)

Depending on interpretation, you might alternatively encode 'powerButton' must occur before StartUpMode, but LTL lacks a 'before' unless explicitly modeling sequences. This formula still enforces that StartUpMode can only be true if powerButton is pressed.

Step by step: 
1. 'Transition to StartUpMode' = StartUpMode
2. 'by pushing the powerButton' = powerButton
3. 'shall be allowed by...' forces the implication StartUpMode -> powerButton
4. 'always', or in all situations, applies the G operator for globally.
So, the final formula is: G(StartUpMode -> powerButton).

The sentence describes that the system can transition to Start-up Mode only if the power button is pressed (to turn on the ventilator). To capture 'the transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button,' we model that whenever StartUpMode becomes true, it is always preceded (at the previous step) by the pressing of the power button. In LTL, a common way to model such causality ("A is only entered if B was the cause") is: whenever StartUpMode occurs, in the previous moment, powerButton was pressed. In standard LTL, there is no 'previous' operator, but a classic encoding is: 'It is always the case that if StartUpMode holds, then powerButton held at the current or immediately previous step.' If you want to capture the causal relationship "powerButton must be pressed to enter StartUpMode, and StartUpMode can only be reached directly after pressing powerButton," you can use: Globally, whenever StartUpMode occurs, it is immediately after powerButton is pressed. In LTL, this is: G(StartUpMode -> X(powerButton)). However, since X means 'next', to say that entering StartUpMode can only occur after powerButton is pressed (i.e., powerButton is pressed in the previous step), you use: G((X StartUpMode) -> powerButton). Alternatively, to informally express 'the only way to reach StartUpMode is by pressing the powerButton,' we can use this causal formulation. Thus, the resulting LTL formula is: G((X(StartUpMode)) -> powerButton).

explanation: The sentence specifies that whenever the power button is pressed, this should allow (i.e., eventually enable) a transition to Start-up Mode (StartUpMode). We express this as: globally, whenever 'powerButton' becomes true, 'StartUpMode' must eventually be true. In LTL, this is G(powerButton -> F StartUpMode).

explanation_dict.natural_language: whenever the power button is pressed, a transition to Start-up Mode is eventually allowed

explanation: First, identify the key propositions: 'transition to Start-up Mode' maps to StartUpMode, and 'pushing the power button ... to turn it on' maps to pressing (powerButton) when the device is off (off). 'shall be allowed by' means that every transition to StartUpMode must be preceded (or enabled) by pressing the powerButton while the system is off. In LTL, this requirement is typically expressed with an implication: it is always (G) true that if the system transitions to StartUpMode, then earlier in the previous step the power button was pressed and the system was off. However, in standard LTL (future-time), causality is best expressed by stating that whenever powerButton and off are true, in the next state it is possible to enter StartUpMode (i.e.: G((off & powerButton) -> X(StartUpMode))). Alternatively, if we want to say that StartUpMode can only be entered via that sequence, we use: G(StartUpMode -> Y(off & powerButton)), where Y is 'previous', but standard LTL does not have 'previous', so the causal approach is better. Thus, the LTL: G((off & powerButton) -> X(StartUpMode)), which states that whenever the system is off and the powerButton is pressed, it is possible for the next state to be StartUpMode. Optionally, we can restrict that StartUpMode can only be reached this way: G(StartUpMode -> Y(off & powerButton)) (requires past operators), but omitting this, we prefer the former since the user requests future-only LTL.

explanation_dict.natural_language: pushing the power button located on the back side of the ventilator unit to turn it on
transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button

explanation:
The sentence essentially says: 'The transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button located on the back side of the ventilator unit to turn it on.' In formalization, this means that whenever the system is off and the power button is pressed, it is possible to (eventually) reach StartUpMode. 

Step-by-step:
- 'The transition to Start-up Mode ... by pushing the power button' means pressing the power button enables, not forces, but allows the possibility of entering StartUpMode. 
- 'to turn it on' + ventilator is 'off' = system starts from 'off' state.
- In temporal logic, 'whenever off and powerButton' then 'eventually StartUpMode'. This is rendered as globally, if (off & powerButton) then eventually StartUpMode.
- This expresses that from any point in time, whenever the system is off and power button is pressed, it is possible to eventually enter StartUpMode.
- The core formula is: G((off & powerButton) -> F StartUpMode)

This does not assert that StartUpMode always follows, but that it is always possible whenever power is pressed from 'off'.

explanation_dict.natural_language: the system can be brought to StartUpMode
whenever off and powerButton, eventually StartUpMode

explanation: The sentence describes the system's behavior for startup: the system transitions to Start-up Mode if and only if the power button is pushed (i.e., pressed). We interpret this as: whenever a transition into StartUpMode occurs, this must have been directly caused by a powerButton press; equivalently, it is not possible for StartUpMode to occur unless there was a previous powerButton press. In LTL, this is expressed by stating that going into StartUpMode is always preceded by a powerButton press, or more commonly, that globally (G), whenever StartUpMode becomes true, then previously (the last input) powerButton must have been pressed. However, because standard LTL (with only X, U, F, G, etc.) does not include 'previous', we typically use implication over time: Globally, if StartUpMode occurs, then previously (in the last transition) powerButton must have been pressed. With only 'next' (X), we rephrase: whenever powerButton is pressed, at some future point, the system enters StartUpMode; and, furthermore, to enforce that StartUpMode cannot happen spontaneously (without the button), we write: G(StartUpMode -> Previously powerButton pressed). Since 'previous' is not allowed, we instead enforce forward causality: G(powerButton -> F(StartUpMode)). This says whenever powerButton is pressed, StartUpMode follows eventually (but does not strictly prohibit StartUpMode without powerButton; if only the allowed path is through the button, this is often sufficient for specs).

explanation: The requirement states that the transition to Start-up Mode should be allowed if the user pushes the power button to turn the ventilator on. This can be interpreted as: whenever the power button is pressed ('powerButton'), it is possible (in some future) to transition to Start-up Mode ('StartUpMode'). In LTL, this is typically specified as: "Globally, if 'powerButton' is true, then eventually 'StartUpMode' becomes true," or G(powerButton -> F StartUpMode).

explanation:
The sentence describes that the system can transition to Start-up Mode by pressing the power button located on the back, which means turning on the unit. This can be rephrased in temporal logic as: If the power button is pressed while the device is off, then eventually the system will enter Start-up Mode. 

We encode this as: Globally, whenever (off & powerButton) happens, eventually StartUpMode will hold. This is written as G((off & powerButton) -> F StartUpMode).

This means whenever the power button is pressed, eventually the system should enter Start-up Mode. This means that if the power button is pushed (powerButton), then it is allowed that the system transitions to Start-up Mode (StartUpMode). In terms of LTL, this is most commonly specified by stating: 'Whenever powerButton is pressed, eventually the system shall enter StartUpMode.' Thus, we use G(powerButton -> F StartUpMode).
